Well, yes, that's the point. But then, the object is carried over through request processing and we [do save in DB at the end](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L111).

With these changes we have only **one** call to the DB instead of:
* **3** for successful responses ([#L45](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L45), [#L78](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L78), [#L111](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L111))
* **4** for exceptions ([#L45](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L45), [#L78](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L78), [#L88](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L88), [#L111](https://github.com/aschn/drf-tracking/blob/master/rest_framework_tracking/mixins.py#L111))

I think that this kind of optimizations is not pointless, as we surely want to limit the overhead brought by the tracking.

Unfortunately we do. [Django defines the default argument to be `None`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/ref/files/file/#django.core.files.File.read) but `SpooledTemporaryFile` (and Python `file` objects in general) can't handle the case where the first argument (`size`) is `None`:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ubuntu/gcs_integration/westore/tests.py", line 31, in test_write_to_storage
    self.assertEqual(f.read(), 'storage contents')
  File "/home/ubuntu/gcs_integration/storages/backends/gcloud.py", line 56, in read
    return super(GoogleCloudFile, self).read(num_bytes)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/tempfile.py", line 585, in read
    return self._file.read(*args)
TypeError: an integer is required
```

Instead the argument has to be omitted or set to a negative number.

The reason you haven't run into this in the `s3boto` backend is because [it uses `*args and **kwargs`](https://github.com/jschneier/django-storages/blob/e52a127523fdd5be50bb670ccad566c5d527f3d1/storages/backends/s3boto.py#L134-L137), which means that if the user omits the argument there's no `None` to pass through to the underlying `read()`. I believe if the user _does_ specify `num_bytes=None` s3boto will break, but I haven't tested this.

Yes, it's equivalent, but the point is on how to use it. To use action-based API, user needs to keep track of the training/recording state by themselves, whereas a scope-based API does it for you. This is the difference between a sequence of get and save current state => set state => revert back the state afterwards, versus a simple scope.

Also, imagine that in the model codes you want to do different things based on whether it's training or predicting. You can't do that with action-based API without involving a book keeping for recording status.

So Redash supports different "organizations" and data sources can be assigned to them individually (the Mozilla setup only has one organization, the default one). There is a notion of a "current organization" per request (e.g. /org1/api/.. and /org2/api/..) but since this task is run outside the request cycle, we can't refer to [`current_org`](https://github.com/getredash/redash/blob/25de0b9dab4096711b1d38400b0b4c5a3d03b458/redash/authentication/org_resolving.py#L19) as Redash does all over the place of the request time code. I don't have a suggestion, but just wanted you to be aware that the status API will mix data sources that could be configured to be separate for different organizations users. It's probably a good idea to ask Arik about it when we move this feature upstream.

Adding: I think all the 2.7 trouble are due to travis venv [has python 2.7.9](https://travis-ci.org/scikit-image/scikit-image/jobs/63883427#L98), the virtualenv package in 2.7 seem to be installed by system python and [we get python 2.7.3 in our venv](https://travis-ci.org/scikit-image/scikit-image/jobs/63883427#L1431). Which is fair enough, as we are going to check system installed packages (scipy, matplotlib, etc).

A work around would of course be to use the wheels, with the drawbacks:
1. Testing deps with wheels, not guaranteeing scikit-image will work with minimum requirements from system packages. This might not be as bad as we think, as operating systems do ship pre-releases and report them as stable packages (ref llvm 3.5 on Ubuntu 14.04). A pre release should not be considered the same as the pypi stable release, and on can potentially stumble upon API-changes that break the build.
2. We will need to compile PIL 1.1.7 to wheel, compile it from source every time or drop the PIL dep.
3. Might be more that I'm unaware of...

If I understand your concern correctly, the difference in the sign of the ```offset``` happens for the following reason. While calling ```drawImage```, when you provide an offset value of 0.25, it offsets the centroid of the profile by 0.25 pixels, which corresponds to moving the image boundary by -0.25, which is what you are seeing here. One can pass these offsets while calling ```drawImage``` and pass the resulting images to ```interleaveImage``` and get the original image back. > Edit: I think I might be understanding what you want now sorry, you are saying if the Authenticate header has a value then we should check only in those cases that it returns a 1 or a 3? 

Yes. I am saying that the NTLM header has the message type word, and we only tolerate two values for that word: 1 and 3. However, the specification allows another value (2), and a misbehaving client could generate other values as well. So rather than letting the value pass silently and returning a 401, we should abort the server with a big loud message saying "something super unexpected happened". In other words, we should assert. =)

In practice, this means we want `assert message_type in (None, 1, 3)`.

OK @chemelnucfin so I've actually pulled out my editor and looked at this module wholesale. Final answer: just remove all mention of `\A` and `\Z` (they don't need to be replaced with `^` or `$` either).

Reasoning:

- `_PROJECT_PATTERN` is never a full reg-ex, it's a fragment intended to be used with `_TOPIC_REF_PATTERN`
- It "makes" sense to re-use `_TOPIC_REF_FMT` in this way because `_TOPIC_REF_FMT` is needed in `BucketNotification.create`

I have never seen any code that actually uses `\A` and `\Z`, likely because `\A` and `\Z` are only relevant (as alternatives to `^` and `$`) in multiline mode, and when in multiline mode people don't usually think of "beginning" and "end".

I think this type of abstraction pattern works well to reduce code duplication when all types of "items" are basic key/value pair lists. This is particularly true when there are many item types with only the names of their attributes changing between types.

However this kind of abstraction layer can make a lot of basic UI customizations more difficult:

* change the order in which attributes are displayed
* add additional grouping or formatting to particular sets of attributes
* handle new types of inputs: boolean, multi-select, text area...
* incorporate validation

If these sorts of customization become necessary on a per-item-type basis, it can tip the balance and the extra abstraction layer becomes more complex than just writing a new view customized for each type of item.

What is the real benefit of this? If the module is not installed it will raise `ImportError: No module named pylarion.<submodule>` which states that the pylarion is not available and will already exit the process.

The main point here is that doing like so will mask other import errors, for example you have pylarion installed and some of its dependencies are missing then it will raise an `ImportError` and will be stated that pylarion is not available which is not correct.

This can be confirmed by doing a simple example, create a file `mymodule.py`:

```python
import missingmodule
```

And then test on the python interpreter:

```
>>> try:
...     import mymodule
... except ImportError:
...     print('mymodule is not available')
... 
mymodule is not available
```

That will be a wrong information since the real cause is:

```
>>> import mymodule
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "mymodule.py", line 1, in <module>
    import someother
ImportError: No module named someother
```

> I was actually going to suggest giving all the existing bootstrap scripts version 0, since it's more "special" to human eyes and people will probably give version 1 to new scripts no matter what our documentation says.

I personally have a preference of hard coding a list like this. Up until now, we've had no reason to change our bootstrap scripts and I'm only planning on adding a new one. Rather than hoping/documenting that future devs should do versioning a specific way, I'd rather put this list here so bootstrap script authors don't need to know anything about what version strings are treated specially. This is behavior I originally added to mozharness.  The idea is, `context` is used a lot, and `context.config` and `context.cot_config` as well.  If they're dicts, then they can be modified at runtime easily.  This may be intentional, if we want to change `context.config['foo'] = bar`.  Sometimes this is unintentional: we pass `context.config` to something by reference, and that function makes an unintentional change, and we end up with a modified `context.config` that causes weird behavior later on.

By changing `context.config` and `context.cot_config` to frozendicts with tuples instead of lists as child values, we force someone who wants to make a runtime change to explicitly cast to a dict before they can alter values; this is discouraged anyway, and we'll be more clear that that's what's happening.  Otherwise we're more certain that the runtime config will not change accidentally.

Here I'm computing load once per heartbeat. Currently, load computation is O(n) in the number of *ready* tasks. I don't expect the ready queue size to be large. In fact, I expect it to be controlled by the overcommit factor (currently implicitly set to 1.0 in this PR). In such a case, the size of the ready queue is expected to be bound by (2f-1.0)*node.total. 
I did think about optimizing this by caching the load. This adds complexity, as we need to make sure we keep the load accurate  and update it on each task state transition. I think it will make more sense when (1) I refactor the task queue transitions to be internal to the class (as opposed to external), and (2) when we see this adding performance overhead (in its current form I don't expect it to, given a small bound on the ready queue size.)
